you know, the way we judge our return on investment is fair or unfair.

Which is usually how we're, you know, getting a good feel on the accuracy of the market rent data from a third party.

Uh But alongside of that, I was using it to kind of check the market surveys that were done on site by the property managers and their teams.

It's not my responsibility to have them, you know, change them and a test for the accuracy of them.

Uh You know, we're, we're, we're trying to drill these property teams into, you know, this is a new product you're competing with new properties.
Uh, you know, especially property manager that have been there for maybe 10 years.

They're accustomed to a certain level of rents and there's usually a lot of pushback from the get go, you know, everybody wants a new shiny product, but when it comes to getting the rents that go along with that product, uh, sometimes, you know, we don't have the right team in place and they're trying to suppress rents.

And that's one of the ways that they've done it is by basically just putting in, you know, the lowest price that they're seeing, uh whether it has discounts or not, they, you know, multiplying the, uh the specials that they're seeing.

What I'm basically looking at a high level, you know, the I think the lowest level they're giving is kind of on a floor plan, average rent uh where I was just looking at high level, average rent for a property.
Uh and that number, you know, could be 100 $200 off, could be 506 $100 off, especially with some of my projects that rents were moving up pretty quick.
Uh There was a, it took a lot more months than I imagined to catch up.
Uh So I always knew there was a degree of lag between rents, you know, what the rents actually were, you know, in current day or even current month.
And you know what they're reporting, uh uh from my research analysis.
I think it's more of a, it's closer to a two month lag.
Uh, you know, and that, that's enough time to put us behind the curve.
If we think rents are here and we push rents to there and then they come back with actuals and they're up here, then we're, it's too late, we fall behind market.

And so, you know, if there, if the, if we're not doing accurate and timely market surveys and that on top of it is not telling us that rents are growing like they actually are or conversely dropping less than we think, you know, we're dropping more, they're actually just staying stable.
Uh Yeah, then, then we're falling behind the curve and, and not getting the growth that we need to write this project off as a success.

Uh And so those were the biggest discrepancies.

and that's probably gonna be another five or six.

Uh It's in Savannah, which has been a pretty good market.

It may have just been a capital project, just a normal one where we, you know, throw some paint on the wall and, and put in some furniture and make it a club room for residents.
But there was really no structure change, but there was enough opportunity in that location, Savannah to uh make more of an opportunity to get return on investment on it.

So um maybe one of those examples, if you um can think back to the market assessment that you did specifically for one of those um in that of those of those examples, um I would just love to understand sort of how you approached that market assessment to know like, yes, this is the, the what criteria and questions you asked about that market and which metrics you were particularly interested in understanding, to isolate that.
Yes, this is something that looks good and we want to pursue it or no, you know, maybe next time,
yeah, the number one driving factor that we put first and foremost in our underwriting presentation would be the gap to the top of the market.

Uh So, you know, for example, we made the average rents 1500 you have new supply, you know, properties that are in lease up or got delivered in the last two years, much nicer, you know, they may be going for 2500.
So you have $1000 gap between us and new supply and then you have probably a different ring underneath that, that is a decade old,
you know, a little bit nicer than we are currently, you know, same area.
They may be getting 304 $100 more than we are, then we know that there's a lot of opportunity just in that gap to those two different.
So we'll look at like top 10% which usually is just the new supply, you know, everything that's basically 2020 newer.
And then there'll be like a
50% average.
Kind of, well, we're not really looking at any properties that are underneath us.
So we're basically tagging us as the bottom.
And so we'll look at top and then we'll look at what that average, that next average is.

And if there's still a pretty good gap there, then we know that, that we have like a matrix that basically sorts it and ranks it and that's the, the highest percentage of what brings properties to the top.

And then what we've looked at heavily in the past has been the supply coming in, obviously now is a little different environment than it was what it was last year and the year before where, you know, we're kind of trying to stay away from just a massive amounts of supply like Phoenix and Austin, uh you know, a healthy amount of supply, but not where it's just,
you know, killing demand and you have, you know, negative demand.
And so, uh in the past, we didn't really even look at demand because we were just like all right, new supplies coming in, that means
obviously, these are gonna be much nicer much higher rents.
We have opportunity to raise these rents $200 just by putting in 3 $4 million you know, obviously depending on the size of the property.
But those were there, there are two main uh metrics that we looked at
and for the rent and the gap.
What is enough when you say a big gap?
Like what is there a Yeah, what's the formula that you're looking at to say justify enough?
It really depends because I mean, we've had, we've done some projects where we, the data did not really back up what we thought.
And so, you know, we, we know that we're in a nice area.
